quotes.verbatim_quote:
Diminished 7th chords resolve beautifully into tonic chords. Actually they aren't
diminished chords at all when used in this respect. They are the Upper tones to a dim-
inished or flatted 9th chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational reframing: a diminished 7th is the upper structure of a flat-9 dominant.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In exercise 5la you havea G7b9 chord with the root omitted.
In 51b you have a B Diminished chord. Analyze the two chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Side-by-side comparison proves harmonic equivalence between root-omitted dominant-flat-9 and the diminished chord.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In exercise 52 which is anextraction of exercise 24 in lesson 11, notice how the diminish-
ed chord instead of the dominant, resolves into the tonic. Analyze this thoroughly. This
is another form of the Altered chord that can be used along with those illustrated in ex-
ercise 23 in lesson 10.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practical rule: diminished chord may replace the dominant in a V-I resolution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Augmented chords may beusedin a similar manner. See Exer-

cise 53.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the resolution principle from diminished to augmented chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
These are the 2nd and 3rd bars of Ex. 24 in Lesson No. 11. Here a and b are the equivalent
to the V - I Resolution.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Caption explicitly labels the diminished-to-tonic move as a V-I resolution.
